title: Antalya considers Kaleiçi Yat Limanı revamp to ease cruise ship berth crisis
standfirst: A technical study by Turkey's transport ministry will explore adding berths at Kaleiçi Yat Limanı to relieve congestion caused by cruise and cargo ships sharing Antalya Port.
metaDescription: Turkey's transport ministry studies Kaleiçi Yat Limanı for cruise berths after congestion forced a 335m ship to dock at a free zone quay.
author: Andra Kiirkivi
content:
Antalya is seeking to resolve a shortage of cruise ship berths by potentially upgrading the historic Kaleiçi Yat Limanı, according to local news outlet Akdeniz Gerçek. The move follows repeated congestion at Antalya Port, where cargo and passenger vessels share the same quay.

Technical study launched

İzzet Ünlü, chairman of the Antalya Denizcileşme Platformu, announced that the 6th Regional Directorate of the Ministry of Transport and Infrastructure has officially begun a technical study in the area. Speaking to Akdeniz Gerçek, Ünlü said the ministry has decided to carry out technical work in two separate locations: Demre and Kaleiçi.

The study will be conducted under the “Kaleiçi Yat Limanı Mendirek Rehabilitation Project” and will determine whether an additional quay or berthing platform can be built at the marina. Ünlü stressed that implementation of any project would depend on legal and bureaucratic approval processes.

Cruise-cargo clash

The urgency of the issue was highlighted when the 335-metre cruise ship “Aroya” was forced to dock at the Serbest Bölge (Free Zone) quay because no suitable berth was available at Antalya Port. The incident brought renewed attention to the long-standing conflict between freight traffic and passenger vessels competing for the same limited rıhtım (quay) space.

The proposed revamp of Kaleiçi Yat Limanı aims to adapt the historic marina for cruise traffic while preserving its historical and natural character. The project has received support from Ahmet Öztürk, chairman of the ATSO Assembly, and the Antalya Forum-X Platformu.

Charter-market relevance

If realised, the project would separate cruise operations from cargo handling, potentially freeing up capacity at Antalya Port and improving scheduling predictability for all users, including charter operators who rely on the port for crew changes, provisioning and guest transfers.

Why it matters for charter

For the charter market, a dedicated cruise berth at Kaleiçi Yat Limanı could reduce congestion at Antalya Port, easing logistical bottlenecks for yacht crew changes, provisioning and guest transfers.

sources.title: Kaleiçi Yat Limanı İçin Kruvaziyer Hamlesi - Akdeniz Gerçek

title: Truce in Altea marina dispute over mooring fees until September
standfirst: Marina Greenwich and boat owners agree a ceasefire until 16 September, mediated by the Generalitat's director general of Coasts, Marc García Manzana.
metaDescription: Truce in Altea marina conflict: Marina Greenwich and boat owners pause hostilities until September, mediated by Generalitat's Marc García Manzana.
author: Andra Kiirkivi
content:
A temporary truce has been reached in the conflict at the Puerto Deportivo Campomanes in Altea, according to a confidential agreement seen by Moncloa.com. The deal, mediated by Marc García Manzana, director general of Coasts, Ports and Airports of the Generalitat Valenciana, will last until 16 September.

Terms of the ceasefire

Under the agreement, boat owners will remove a warning sign from the Club Náutico building and refrain from speaking to the press. Marina Greenwich, the company owned by Robert Batouche, will stop interfering with the use of moorings and may continue issuing invoices, but cannot market berths because, according to the Conselleria de Medio Ambiente, new public tariffs must first be approved.

The dialogue table, established two months ago by the Dirección General de Costas, Puertos y Aeropuertos, remains open. The Generalitat insists that, although the dispute is civil, the regional administration must remain vigilant over actions within the concession area.

Background of the dispute

The conflict began when the original 50-year concession (1976, 2026) for Marina Greenwich ended. The company obtained a ten-year extension and argues that this extinguishes the historical rights of mooring holders, who had been paying frozen tariffs well below market rates. Some users had even registered the berths as their property after making payments in the past.

Affected parties have denounced what they call a 'pelotazo' (a quick profit) and estimate the company's potential benefit at around 40 million euros, according to Gonzalo Pons-Trénor, secretary of the Club Náutico Campomanes, writing in an opinion piece. The Generalitat has clarified that, although the extension ends previous rights, Marina Greenwich cannot unilaterally set new tariffs; the Conselleria de Medio Ambiente must approve public prices that should be more moderate.

Wider context

The port has been in the news before. Years ago, Marina Greenwich attempted an expansion that would have damaged the Altea coast; the project was stopped by environmental groups and the Generalitat had to pay 1.8 million euros in compensation. Batouche had claimed 97 million euros but lost in court.

Former minister Eduardo Zaplana, who is serving a sentence for the Erial case, is reported to have had a yacht moored at the port, identified by the UCO as his property, and two homes, according to investigations in the Brugal case. In 2017, Zaplana tried to acquire the concessionaire, according to recordings from the case file.

The truce does not resolve the underlying issues. The next meeting on 16 September will determine whether the conflict ends with an agreement on tariffs or, as mooring holders fear, ends up in court.

Why it matters for charter

This dispute reflects a recurring tension in Spanish marinas when public-land concessions expire, directly affecting charter operators and berth holders who face sudden tariff hikes.

sources.title: La Generalitat Valenciana media una tregua en el puerto deportivo de Altea por el conflicto de amarres de 40 millones - moncloa.com

title: Spain's Charter Licence Is Gone. Here's What Replaced It
standfirst: A decade-old permit regime has given way to a self-declaration filed with the harbour master — but the switchover date has moved three times, and first-time operators still face the old paperwork once.
author: Mardo Soo
metaDescription: Spain replaced the charter licence with a declaracion responsable filed at the harbour master. What changed, when it took effect, and what first-timers still must do.
content:
Anyone who has put a boat into commercial charter in Spain knows the old routine: a trip to the Harbour Master's office, a stack of certificates, and a wait of several weeks before the licencia de chárter came back. That regime is finished. In its place is a declaración responsable — a self-declaration the operator files, taking personal responsibility for the boat's compliance.

The practical effect is that the administration no longer grants permission in advance. It accepts your declaration, and audits you afterwards.

Where the rules come from

The reform sits in Royal Decree 186/2023 of 21 March 2023, which swept away two long-obsolete instruments — the Ministerial Order of 4 December 1985 and the Vessel Clearance Regulations of 2000. Law firm Penningtons Manches Cooper, which advised on the change, notes that the decree also opened Spanish chartering to non-EU flagged yachts regardless of length, putting British-flagged boats on the same footing as EU ones after years of post-Brexit friction.

The timetable was then amended by Royal Decree 587/2024 of 25 June 2024.

The date moved three times

This is where operators have been caught out. Penningtons recorded the new dispatch and clearance regime as effective from 1 July 2024. Royal Decree 587/2024 subsequently pushed the definitive start to 15 August 2025. SuperyachtNews reports that the system only became fully operational in 2026, once the maritime administration's software had caught up.

If you have been told three different start dates by three different brokers, that is why. All three were correct at the time they were given.

What the declaration actually commits you to

Filing the affidavit with the Harbour Master's office is what authorises the vessel to charter in Spain. In signing it, the operator asserts that the boat holds valid technical certificates, that crew documentation is in order, that mandatory insurance is in force, and that the required maritime certifications are held.

Nothing on that list is new. What has changed is who carries the risk of getting it wrong. Under the old licence, the administration checked before saying yes. Under the declaration, you assert compliance and bear the consequences if the assertion fails an inspection.

First-timers still do it the old way — once

There is a transitional trap worth knowing. According to SuperyachtNews, an operator chartering in Spain for the first time in 2026 must still go through the old procedure to get the vessel's data onto the system, which yields a three-month licence. One day before that licence expires, the operator files the affidavit — and from then on sits permanently in the new system.

So the answer to "do I still need a charter licence in Spain?" is: not if you are already registered, and once if you are not.

The flag on the backstay

Trade coverage of the reform also points to an identification requirement that catches the eye from the pontoon: charter vessels flying the international Maritime Code flag "H" (Hotel) to mark the boat as operating commercially. Reporting on the package further indicates that the charter's operating location must be approved by the relevant harbour authority, buoy operator or coastal authority, with changes of location notified in advance, alongside tighter documentation of everyone on board.

Why this matters if you are just booking a day out

For a guest, none of this changes the booking. It does, however, give you a sharper question to ask. A compliant operator can tell you without hesitation which regime their boat sits under and show current insurance. One that cannot is worth passing on.

It also explains a pattern in the market. Skippered charter shifts the licensing and compliance burden onto a professional who does this daily — which is a large part of why so little of the Spanish fleet is offered bareboat. We looked at what that share actually is in our fleet data for July 2026.

If you are weighing whether you need a qualification of your own, our guide on whether you need a licence to rent a boat in Spain covers the recreational side.

sources.title: Spain replaces 'charter licence' with self-declaration system
New law set to positively impact yachts and the charter market in Spain

title: Puerto Sotogrande Is Now Spain's Fifth-Largest Marina, Ahead of Puerto Banús
standfirst: A new count of active water berths puts Sotogrande's 1,382 at the head of every Andalusian marina — and reorders the Costa del Sol pecking order in the process.
author: Andra Kiirkivi
metaDescription: Puerto Sotogrande ranks fifth among Spain's largest marinas with 1,382 water berths, leading all Andalusian ports ahead of Benalmadena and Puerto Banus.
content:
Puerto Sotogrande has been ranked the fifth-largest marina in Spain by number of active water berths, according to a classification compiled by the Observatorio de Marinas y Puertos with data current to 14 July 2026 and reported on 21 July.

The Cádiz-province marina counts 1,382 operational berths. That places it at the head of every Andalusian facility in the table — ahead of Puerto Deportivo Benalmádena, Puerto Deportivo Almerimar, Puerto Banús and Puerto Sherry.

The national top five

  1. Marina d'Empuriabrava — 5,000 berths
  2. Port de la Ràpita — 1,825 berths
  3. Puerto Deportivo Tomás Maestre — 1,721 berths
  4. Marina dels Canals de Santa Margarida — 1,598 berths
  5. Puerto Sotogrande — 1,382 berths

Empuriabrava's margin is a quirk of geography rather than a fair fight: the Costa Brava development is built around a residential canal network, so a large share of its berths are private moorings at the bottom of gardens.

What the count included — and what it left out

The methodology matters here, because marina capacity figures are routinely inflated. The Observatorio counted active water berths only. Dry storage, shipyards, parking and announced-but-unbuilt expansions were all excluded, with figures taken from official marina websites and port authorities.

That is a stricter basis than the numbers marinas tend to quote in their own marketing, and it is why some familiar names sit lower than expected.

Why size is not the same as charter capacity

A berth count measures how many boats a marina can hold, not how many are available to charter. Sotogrande's basin is dominated by privately owned yachts kept on annual contracts; Puerto Banús, smaller in this ranking, remains the Costa del Sol's busiest commercial departure point because a far greater share of its fleet works.

For anyone planning a day on the water, the practical question is which port you leave from, not which has the most pontoons. Our guide to choosing between Marbella and Puerto Banús as a departure port covers the trade-offs, and the current fleet is at boat rental Marbella.

Puerto Banús placed second in a separate 2026 ranking judged on services and prestige rather than raw capacity — covered here.

sources.title: Puerto Sotogrande claims fifth spot among Spain's largest marinas

title: Puerto Banús Named Spain's Second-Best Marina for 2026
standfirst: The nautical platform Velvua puts Marbella's 915-berth harbour behind only Real Club Náutico de Palma, judging on services, connectivity, prestige and price transparency.
author: Andra Kiirkivi
metaDescription: Velvua ranks Puerto Banus second among Spain's best marinas for 2026, behind Real Club Nautico de Palma, citing its 915 berths and 50-metre maximum length.
content:
Puerto Banús has been ranked the second-best marina in Spain for 2026 by the nautical platform Velvua, placing behind only the Real Club Náutico de Palma in a table covering leading marinas on the Spanish mainland and in the Balearics.

Velvua assessed marinas on capacity and maximum vessel length, quality of services, connectivity and historical prestige, and pricing transparency. The platform described Puerto Banús as one of the most internationally recognisable marina brands on the Costa del Sol, pointing to its combination of nautical facilities with dining, tourism and entertainment.

The numbers behind the ranking

  • 915 berths
  • Vessels accommodated up to 50 metres in length
  • Reported growth in demand for boats over 30 metres

That last point is the one to watch. Demand shifting toward the 30-metre-plus bracket is a berth-allocation problem as much as a commercial opportunity: every large yacht that takes a berth displaces more than one mid-size boat, and Puerto Banús has a fixed footprint.

Two rankings, two different questions

Read alongside the Observatorio de Marinas y Puertos capacity table published a day earlier — in which Puerto Banús sits behind Puerto Sotogrande among Andalusian marinas — the Velvua result is a useful corrective.

Counting berths tells you how big a marina is. Judging services, connectivity and transparency tells you what it is like to use. Puerto Banús does not win on size, and does not need to: its advantage is that a charter guest can arrive from Málaga airport in under an hour, walk to the boat, and find somewhere to eat afterwards.

What it means for charter guests

Price transparency appearing in the criteria is worth noting, because opacity has long been the Costa del Sol's weak point — quoted charter rates that exclude fuel, or a skipper fee that surfaces at the pontoon.

Our current fleet departing the port is listed at yacht charter Puerto Banús, and if you are deciding when to go, our guide to the best time to charter in Marbella covers the seasonal wind pattern.

title: 89% of Charter Boats Come With a Skipper — and in Spain It's 98%
standfirst: A snapshot of 483 active listings across 23 countries shows bareboat has become the exception, instant booking is close to non-existent, and the median charter yacht is under 14 metres.
author: Mardo Soo
metaDescription: BoatHire24 fleet data, July 2026: 89% of 483 active charter listings include a skipper, rising to 98% in Spain. Only 3.5% offer instant booking.
content:
We publish our own inventory figures periodically because the charter market has very little public data in it. What follows is a snapshot of every active listing on BoatHire24 as of 22 July 2026 — 483 boats across 23 countries. Method is at the foot of the piece.

Bareboat has become the exception

430 of 483 active boats (89.0%) include a skipper. Just 53 listings — 11.0% — are offered bareboat.

In Spain the concentration is sharper still: 96 of 98 Spanish listings (98.0%) come with a skipper. Two boats in the entire Spanish fleet can be taken out on your own.

This is not an accident of supply. It tracks the compliance burden. Under the reformed Spanish regime, the operator files a self-declaration asserting that certificates, insurance and crew documentation are all in order — and carries the risk if they are not. Putting a professional skipper aboard moves that risk to someone who handles it daily. We covered the rule change in Spain's charter licence is gone.

Instant booking barely exists

Only 17 of 483 boats (3.5%) support instant booking. The remaining 96.5% require the owner or operator to confirm.

Charter is roughly two decades behind hotels here. The reasons are real — weather cancellations, skipper availability, tide and berth constraints — but the gap between "book a room in ten seconds" and "wait for a reply about a boat" is the single clearest piece of friction left in the industry, and it is ours to fix rather than explain.

The median charter yacht is smaller than the brochures suggest

Of the 376 listings with a recorded length, the median is 13.9 metres, spanning 3.0 m to 52.0 m. Just under a third — 123 boats, 32.7% — are under 12 metres.

Marketing imagery skews heavily toward superyachts. The market is mostly day-boats.

Fleet composition

  • Motor yacht — 218
  • Catamaran — 86
  • Speedboat — 59
  • Sailing — 53
  • RIB — 28
  • Gulet — 19

By country, the fleet is led by Spain (98), Thailand (97), Turkey (64), France (46) and Croatia (31). Spain accounts for 20.3% of all active listings.

Method and limitations

Figures are counted from BoatHire24's live listings database on 22 July 2026, restricted to boats with an active status. Length statistics exclude the 107 listings with no recorded length overall, so the median covers 376 boats rather than all 483. Percentages are rounded to one decimal place.

The obvious limitation: this is one marketplace, not the whole market. It over-represents the destinations where we have most supply and under-represents large bareboat fleets in the Balearics and Croatia, where flotilla operators dominate. Treat the direction as sound and the absolute levels as indicative.

The full live fleet is at explore boats.

sources.title: Spain replaces 'charter licence' with self-declaration system
